message.content:
Try HARD to REFUTE this architecture through ONE lens. Default refuted=true if uncertain. Check /home/polaris/wt/flywheel, /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board, and /home/polaris/polaris_project/sota_review/foundation if needed.

THESIS: Faithfulness is a property of NAMES, not of SENTENCES. Fabrication is always the introduction of a PARTICULAR — a number, an entity, a study, a date, an attribution — or the binding of a proposition to a source that does not assert it. It is NEVER the assertion of a RELATION. So we delete span-grounding (a per-sentence entailment gate that I measured deleting 93% of the #1 system's own prose) and replace it with a CLOSED-WORLD REFERENTIAL CONTRACT: you may reason freely, you may never name freely. Everything the judge pays for lives in relations; everything fraud consists of lives in particulars; separating those two is the whole system.
DESIGN: ## PART 0 — THE MEASUREMENTS THIS DESIGN RESTS ON (I ran these tonight; they are not inherited)

**M1. Our own contract deletes 93% of the #1 system's prose.**
I extracted cellcog's task-72 artifact (16,334 words), split it into paragraphs, bound anaphora runs, and isolated its 316 reviewer-voice sentences. Then I applied our OWN deterministic gates from `/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/scripts/synthesis_contract.py`:

```
cellcog reviewer-voice sentences: 316
  DELETED by NO_VERDICT_VOCAB    294  (93%)
  DELETED by UNIVERSAL             4
  DELETED by CAUSAL_IMPORT         4
  DELETED by FORECAST              4
>>> TOTAL DELETED BY OUR CONTRACT: 295/316 = 93%
```
- `VERDICT_VOCAB` (gate 8, error string `no_verdict_vocabulary (this is a vibe, not an adjudication)`) does 93% of the damage BY ITSELF. It requires every sentence to contain one of ~17 hardcoded English idioms. It is not a faithfulness gate. It is a style gate calibrated against nothing.
- `UNIVERSAL` (`synthesis_contract.py`, regex `\b(all|every|always|never|none|no one|...|proves)\b`) deletes cellcog's THESIS SENTENCE: *"Institutional mediation **proves** decisive across five levels."*
- `FORECAST` (bans the word `will`) deletes cellcog's entire future-research agenda.
- `CAUSAL_IMPORT` deletes its adjudications: *"The German pattern is not accidental; it **reflects** coordinated bargaining, works councils, and the dual vocational-education system."*

**And our gate rejects the OPERATOR'S OWN PRESCRIBED SENTENCES.** I verified both:
- Fix 5's mandated corpus-scoped gap form — *"Within the 137 journal articles retrieved for this review, **none** measures X"* — is REJECTED by `UNIVERSAL` (matches `none`).
- The insight rubric's own w=0.15 criterion *"Value and Foresight in Delineating Implications and Future Research Agendas"* is unwritable: *"Future research **will** need to..."* is REJECTED by `FORECAST`.

**We built a gate that deletes the rubric.** This is the finding. It is not the corpus that stands between us and the top — it is a hundred lines of regex.

**M2. The #1 system is ALREADY referentially closed — which is why my primitive is a description, not a hypothesis.**
Of cellcog's 316 reviewer-voice sentences, only **12 (3.8%)** contain a particular (number or proper noun) that appears nowhere in its cited prose, headings, tables, or reference list. I inspected all 12: every one is an artifact of my crude extractor — decade expressions ("the early 2000s", "post-1970s"), and anaphora runs my regex failed to bind ("Rather than matching patents to tasks, **they** linked AI capability categories from the Electronic Frontier Foundation's dataset" — that IS an attributed sentence). **The true rate of unregistered particulars in cellcog's own-voice prose is effectively ZERO.**

cellcog asserts original mechanisms across **64% of its sentences (316 of 495)** and it never once names a thing it did not get from a source. That is the exact contract I am proposing. The #1 system already runs it. We are the only ones enforcing entailment.

**M3. The atom is not the sentence — it is the RUN.**
Binding anaphora ("They estimated an 11 percent within-firm wage premium...") moved 179 sentences from "uncited" to "attributed." cellcog's evidence reporting comes in **attribution runs**: one citation, then several sentences of texture bound to it by anaphora. Sol makes the sentence the atom ("a STRUCTURED SENTENCE LEDGER"); Fable makes the sentence the atom ("a sentence may assert exactly one of four things"). **Both are wrong, and being wrong about this is the root cause of BOTH our failures at once:** a per-sentence grounding gate forces per-sentence citation, which is why we have 240 `[n]` markers and a 677-word median paragraph and the worst readability on the board (0.3774). Fix the atom and you fix faithfulness AND readability with one change.

**M4. The arithmetic (Sol's, verified) and one thing Sol missed.**
Score = `target/(target+reference)` (`third_party/deep_research_bench/deepresearch_bench_race.py:155-170`) against a FIXED 9,029-word reference (`data/test_data/cleaned_data/reference.jsonl`). Points multiples needed:
```
OVERALL  now 0.780R  need 1.261R  => x1.62
INSIGHT  now 0.736R  need 1.330R  => x1.81
READ     now 0.606R  need 1.081R  => x1.78   <-- nobody noticed this
COMPREH  now 0.835R  need 1.290R  => x1.55
INSTR    now 0.789R  need 1.237R  => x1.57
```
**Readability needs almost the same points multiple as insight (1.78x vs 1.81x) — and it is the only one that is purely mechanical.** Sol books +0.021 for it and moves on. It is the highest-confidence lever on the board and it is currently the SECOND-biggest points hole.

---

## PART 1 — THE RUBRIC IS DERIVED, NOT READ (the generality keystone, made sharper)

`scripts/rubric_derive.py` (new).

The operator's keystone is right and I verified the mechanism: `third_party/deep_research_bench/prompt/criteria_prompt_en.py` contains `generate_eval_dimension_weight_prompt` and `generate_eval_criteria_prompt_{comp,insight,instruction,readability}` — **every one is handed ONLY `{task_prompt}`.** The output is cached at `data/criteria_data/criteria.jsonl` (100 rows: `id`, `prompt`, `dimension_weight`, `criterions`). Task 72's row gives the four weights (.29/.32/.25/.14) and 25 weighted criteria (6+5+7+7).

**My addition: the generator is STOCHASTIC, so derive the rubric k=5 times and take the ENSEMBLE.**
- Criteria appearing in ≥3/5 samples are STABLE — these are the real axes of the question.
- Criteria appearing 1–2/5 are VOLATILE — cover them cheaply, don't build sections around them.
- Compose against the union, weighted by (frequency × weight).

This converts "guess the rubric" into "measure the rubric's distribution," costs 5 cheap LLM calls, and is strictly a better estimator than one sample. Sol and Fable both READ the cached task-72 criteria file by hand — that IS the overfit the operator caught.

**GATE 0 (free, run first):** derive task-72's rubric from its prompt and diff 
WHY GENERAL: The test is: which constants survive a change of domain? I walk two queries end to end. **Nothing in the pipeline mentions AI, labour, myeloma, or 2008.**

---
### QUERY A (clinical): *"CAR-T vs bispecific antibodies in relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma"*

1. **RUBRIC DERIVE (k=5 ensemble):** same generator (`criteria_prompt_en.py`), handed only this prompt. It will emit different weights (insight and comprehensiveness likely rise; instruction-following likely falls, because this prompt imposes almost no constraints) and different criteria — plausibly "Coverage of pivotal trials", "Comparative efficacy and safety", "Sequencing and access", "Patient selection". **DERIVED.**
2. **SCOPE DERIVE:** `venue_class = peer_reviewed` — **NOT journal_only, because this prompt does not say so.** This is where Sol's and Fable's task-72 machinery would silently misfire: they bake journal-only into the engine. Here it must not fire. `recency.from = 2019` (first CAR-T approvals) — **derived from the topic's technology clock, not typed.** `language = any`.
3. **SEEDS:** highest `is-referenced-by-count` works passing the topic gate → KarMMa, CARTITUDE-1, MajesTEC-1, MonumenTAL-1. **Found by citation count, not hand-named.** Citation-graph expansion then reaches the mechanism and toxicity literature that keyword search misses.
4. **LEDGER:** numbers = ORR 73%, ORR 97%, mPFS 8.8 mo, grade≥3 CRS 4%. Entities = ide-cel, cilta-cel, teclistamab, talquetamab, BCMA, GPRC5D. **Every one has a card id and a character offset.**
5. **CONTRADICTION INDEX:** CARTITUDE-1 ORR 97% vs KarMMa ORR 73%. DIMENSION DIFF computed over the typed fields: prior lines of therapy, patient fitness, manufacturing/bridging window.
6. **MASTER KEY (computed, not authored):** the dimension resolving the most contradiction pairs will very likely come out as *prior lines / patient fitness / who survives to infusion*. The thesis writes itself from the data: **"apparent efficacy differences between CAR-T and bispecifics are substantially a function of the population that survives to treatment."** Reviewer voice, marked `[Analytical Hypothesis — Our Synthesis]`, referentially closed, anchored to a block whose premises are the two trials. **Legal under my contract. Deleted by our current one** (`VERDICT_VOCAB`, no verdict idiom).
7. **FRAME:** the prompt's frame is "relapsed/refractory". Critique-and-subordinate: is that category stable as lines of therapy multiply? Contest it with cited sources.
8. **GAP:** *"Within the 118 articles retrieved for this review, none reports a head-to-head randomised comparison."* **True by construction** — read off an empty matrix cell and the corpus audit.

---
### QUERY B (historical): *"What caused the 2008 financial crisis?"*

1. **RUBRIC DERIVE:** insight almost certainly dominates (causal explanation *is* the task); instruction-following drops (almost no constraints to follow). **The weights move, and they move because they are derived.**
2. **SCOPE DERIVE:** `ve
FAITHFULNESS: ## THE LINE

**FABRICATION — remains IMPOSSIBLE, and is now enforced by two mechanisms that CANNOT silently fail:**
1. **Introducing a PARTICULAR that does not exist in the evidence.** Blocked by REFERENTIAL CLOSURE: deterministic set membership against a ledger built from fetched source text. Every number, name, venue, year and study in the report resolves to a card id and a character offset. No LLM in the loop.
2. **Binding a proposition to a source that does not assert it.** Blocked by KIND-A entailment + Fable's attribution-binding gate + my new run-purity sub-gate.

**SCHOLARSHIP — is now LEGAL:** any relation, mechanism, adjudication, taxonomy, punchline, gap or agenda, asserted in the reviewer's marked voice, over registered particulars, anchored to a block with premises.

## WHAT WE KEEP (and why each protects TRUST)
- **Extractive-only card fields** (Fable). The LLM selects offsets; it never composes spans. This is the only reason the ledger can be trusted at all.
- **Mechanism pool = the sentence's own cited cards** (Fable's correction, which caught a real hole in the operator's first formulation) — re-expressed as a **type discipline on runs** rather than a substring pool over 150 cards, which is what made it degenerate into "does the sentence contain a word the field uses."
- **ATTACK #1 as a CI canary.** It stays REJECTED, unchanged, under my architecture: it is an attributed run whose proposition is not entailed by the span it cites.
- **Corpus-scoped gap claims** (Fable / Fix 5) — and now *mechanically* true, generated from empty matrix cells with the count read from the corpus audit.
- **The poisoned-card CI test** and the hard abort: one fabricated particular on the page and the artifact is burned regardless of score.
- **Hedge + tag is NOT a license** (Fable, and she is right). But I locate the reason precisely: a license is needed for a **fact**, not for an **opinion** — and the thing that distinguishes them is not whether the verb is hedged, it is whether the sentence NAMES something new. Referential closure is what makes hedge+tag not-costume, because a run that names nothing new *cannot assert a novel fact about the world.* It can only assert a relation among things we have evidence for.

## WHAT WE DROP (and why each is cost, not protection)
- **`VERDICT_VOCAB`** — deletes **294 of cellcog's 316 own-voice sentences (93%)**. It is a 17-item English idiom list masquerading as a safety gate. It protects against nothing.
- **`UNIVERSAL`*

LENS:
FEASIBILITY on THIS box: OpenAlex 429s our IP. Semantic Scholar 404s. ~50% of paywalled papers have no OA full text (we recovered 36/70; real quotable full text is ~10). A full compose is ~65 minutes. Judge SD is 0.0074. Is this plan a wish?

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch
